1: Standard messenger formula introducing a new oracle collection. Chapter 8 contains ten distinct oracles each beginning with koh amar YHWH tzvaot (thus says the LORD of Hosts). The repetition of the formula structures the chapter as a deliberate collection of restoration promises, counterbalancing the judgments and calls to repentance in chapters 1-7. The divine name YHWH tzvaot (LORD of Hosts) is particularly dense in Zechariah — used more than 50 times in the book, emphasizing the universal sovereignty of YHWH over all powers as the ground for the restoration promises.
2: qane qineti leTziyon qinah gedolah vechemath gedolah qaneti lah: ‘I am burning with jealousy for Zion — great jealousy — and I am stirred with great wrath because of her.’ Qanna/qinah is the divine jealousy that belongs to the covenant relationship — YHWH’s passionate, exclusive commitment to his people (Exod 20:5; 34:14). The term covers both intense love and fierce protectiveness: the jealousy is not petty possessiveness but the burning energy of covenantal fidelity. The doubled construction (great jealousy, great wrath) intensifies the intensity: YHWH is not mildly interested in Zion but passionately committed to her restoration. The wrath (chemah) is directed at those who have oppressed her, not at her herself — a signal that the restoration era is beginning.
3: shavti el Tziyon veshachanti betoch Yerushalayim venikra Yerushalayim ir ha-emet vehar YHWH tzvaot har ha-qodesh: ‘I have returned to Zion and will live in Jerusalem. Jerusalem will be called the City of Truth, and the mountain of the LORD of Hosts will be called the Holy Mountain.’ The two titles given to the restored Jerusalem define the book’s ethical vision: ir ha-emet (city of truth/faithfulness, from emet) and har ha-qodesh (holy mountain). Emet (truth/faithfulness/reliability) becomes the signature virtue of the restored community in this chapter (8:3, 8:16, 8:19). The verb shavti (I have returned) is the same root as the call to the people to “return” to YHWH (1:3): YHWH’s return to Zion is the mirror of the community’s return to YHWH.
4: od yeshvu zeqenim uzeqenot birechovot Yerushalayim: ‘Old men and old women will once again sit in the streets of Jerusalem, each one leaning on a staff because of great age.’ The image of the elderly sitting safely in Jerusalem’s streets is a picture of civic shalom in its most concrete form — the city is at peace, there is no fear of enemy attack, and the aged are not driven from their homes. Old age was a symbol of YHWH’s blessing (Prov 16:31: grey hair is a crown of glory); a city without the elderly was a city stripped of its blessing. The specific detail of the staff (mishenet) because of great age adds verisimilitude: this is not idealized imagery but a picture of actual peaceable life in a well-ordered community.
5: urechovot ha-ir yimale'u yeladim veyeladot mesachaqim birechovoteyha: ‘The streets of the city will be full of boys and girls playing in the streets.’ Children playing safely in city streets is the counterpart to the elderly sitting safely: together they represent the full age-range of the community living in security. The specific verb mesachaqim (playing) is significant — it is the same root used in Genesis 21:9 (Ishmael “playing” with Isaac), in Judges 16:25 (Samson “performing” for the Philistines), and in 2 Samuel 6:5 (Israel “celebrating” before the ark). The picture is of uninhibited joy in public space — the transformation of the city into a place of life and celebration rather than fear and mourning.
6: ki yippale beeinei she’erit ha’am hazeh bayamim hahem gam beeinai yippale: ‘Even if this seems impossible to the remnant of this people in those days, will it also seem impossible to me?’ The key word is pele (wonderful/miraculous/impossible) — the same root used in Isaiah 9:6 (Wonderful Counselor) and Judges 13:18 (the angel’s name is wonderful). YHWH’s rhetorical question challenges the community’s limited imagination about what is possible: human impossibility is not divine impossibility. The word pele marks the boundary between what humans can accomplish and what YHWH can accomplish — restoration is in the realm of the wonderful, but YHWH specializes in the wonderful.
7: hineni moshi’a et ami me’eretz mizrach ume’eretz mevo hashemesh: ‘I will rescue/save my people from the lands of the east and the lands of the west.’ Moshi’a (the participle of yasha, to save/deliver) — the same root as the names Joshua (Yehoshua) and Jesus (Yeshua). YHWH describes himself with the word that will become Christ’s name. The universal gathering — from east and west (literally “the rising of the sun” and “the setting of the sun”) — encompasses all directions, all exile locations. This universal ingathering pattern (east and west, or all four directions) is the OT template for the NT’s eschatological gathering from all nations (Matt 8:11; Rev 7:9).
8: veheveti otam veshachanu betoch Yerushalayim vehayu li le’am va’ani ehyeh lahem le’elohim be’emet uvatzedaqah: ‘I will bring them home to live in Jerusalem. They will be my people, and I will be their God, in faithfulness and righteousness.’ The covenant formula (ammi / eloheihem — my people / their God) recurs throughout Scripture from Exodus 6:7 through Jeremiah 31:33 to Revelation 21:3. Here it is qualified with two terms: emet (truth/faithfulness/reliability) and tzedaqah (righteousness/right-relationship). The covenant will be kept with integrity (emet) and in right-relationship (tzedaqah) — both terms pointing to the character of YHWH that grounds the covenant’s permanence.
9: techazaqnah yedeichem: ‘Let your hands be strong.’ The call to strengthen weak hands (chazaq yadayim) echoes Ezra 6:22 and the Nehemiah narrative (Neh 2:18: “let us rise up and build”) — the physical act of rebuilding the temple requires both courage and divine assurance. Isaiah 35:3-4 uses the same idiom in an eschatological register: “Strengthen the weak hands, and make firm the feeble knees ... your God will come with vengeance ... he will come and save you.” Zechariah is consciously linking the current temple-building community with the broader eschatological promise of divine salvation.
10: lifnei hayamim hahem: ‘Before those days.’ The contrast between the pre-temple period (economic hardship, no safety, enemies on every side) and the current restoration era underscores the theological principle that the presence of YHWH’s house in the community transforms its material situation. The economic language — wages, security for travelers, enemies — reflects a comprehensive understanding of shalom: the absence of YHWH’s active presence affects every domain of life, not just the religious. The word shelach (to send out/dispatch, used for those going in and out) suggests commercial activity — the restoration includes economic normalcy.
11: lo chen la’am haze kashenim harishoneem: ‘But from now on I will not deal with the remnant of this people as I did in the past.’ The emphatic contrast — past dealings vs. future dealings — is YHWH’s declaration of a new era. The phrase ka-yamim harishonim (as in the former days) marks the boundary: the era of covenant discipline that scattered and impoverished is over; the era of restoration is beginning. This transition language is the OT’s typical structure for the move from judgment to restoration: “formerly ... but now” (Isa 43:18-19: “Remember not the former things ... I am doing a new thing”; Jer 31:31-32: “not like the covenant I made with their fathers”).
12: ki zera hashalom: ‘For there will be a sowing of peace / a seed of peace.’ The phrase zera hashalom (seed/sowing of peace) is a brilliant agricultural metaphor for comprehensive well-being: peace (shalom) is planted like seed that germinates into vine-fruit, ground-produce, and heavenly dew. The vine, ground, and sky producing together is the classic covenantal prosperity (Deut 11:14; Hos 2:21-22), here presented as the fruit of a planted shalom-seed. The comprehensive character of biblical shalom — material, relational, cosmic — is captured in this single agricultural image.
13: uch’asher heyitem qelalah bagoyim beit Yehudah uveit Yisrael ken oshi’a etchem viheiyitem berachah: ‘Just as you, house of Judah and house of Israel, were a curse among the nations, so I will save you, and you will be a blessing.’ The reversal formula — qelalah (curse) turned to berachah (blessing) — echoes the Abraham promise (Gen 12:2-3: you will be a blessing; whoever curses you I will curse). The exile scattered Israel as a byword and object lesson of divine judgment (Deut 28:37: you will become a horror, a proverb, and a byword); the restoration reverses this: the nations will bless themselves by Israel’s name. The “house of Judah and house of Israel” together (not just the returned southern exiles) points to a reunification of the divided kingdom in the restoration.
14: kammasher zamam YHWH tzvaot la’asot lanu: ‘Just as I determined to bring disaster upon you when your ancestors provoked me to anger.’ The word zamam (purposed/determined) is the key term — it is a strong word for deliberate divine intention. YHWH’s past determination to punish and current determination to bless are presented as equally firm acts of divine will: just as the judgment was not accidental but purposeful, so the restoration is not tentative but purposed. The symmetry of “just as I purposed to harm ... so I have purposed to do good” (vv.14-15) is the covenant’s two-edged character: the same sovereign will that brought discipline now brings blessing.
15: shuv zamam ba-yamim ha-eleh la’asot tov liYerushalayim uveit Yehudah: ‘In these days I have determined again to do good to Jerusalem and the house of Judah. Do not be afraid.’ The double use of zamam (purposed) in vv.14-15 is the theological heart of the passage: YHWH’s purposes — whether in judgment or in restoration — are reliable precisely because they are purposed, not reactive. The closing command al tira’u (do not be afraid) is the consequence of YHWH’s determined purpose: there is no need for fear when the sovereign God has expressed his deliberate intention to do good. This is the OT ground for the NT’s “the Lord is my helper; I will not fear” (Heb 13:6).
16: devarim asher ta’asu dab’ru emet ish et re’ehu mishpat emet veshalom shiftu bish’areichem: ‘These are the things you must do: Speak the truth to one another. Render honest judgments that lead to peace in your gates.’ The two commands are emet (truth/faithfulness in speech) and mishpat emet veshalom (judgment of truth-and-peace in the city gates). Both combine emet (truth/reliability) as the core virtue. The gates were the public square where legal disputes were adjudicated (Ruth 4:1-11; Amos 5:10-15); honest gate-justice was the OT’s primary institutional accountability mechanism. The pairing of truth and peace (shalom) is the book’s ethical formula: shalom grows from emet, not the reverse.
17: ve’ish et ra’at re’ehu al tachashvu bilvavechem ushvuat sheqer al te’ehavu: ‘Do not plot evil against your neighbor in your heart, and do not love perjury.’ The two prohibitions address interior disposition (tachashvu bilvavechem, plot in your hearts) and covenant testimony (shvuat sheqer, false oath). The heart-plotting and the perjury together constitute the two forms of relational dishonesty that destroy community from within: private malice and public lying. The phrase ki et kulam eleh sane’ti (for all these things I hate, declares the LORD) uses sane (hate) — the same strong term of divine aversion used in the Proverbs list of what YHWH hates (Prov 6:16-19), where both “a false witness who breathes out lies” and “one who sows discord among brothers” appear.
18: Transitional oracle formula. The word of the LORD coming “to me” (elai) is the standard prophetic reception formula, signaling a new oracle unit within the larger collection. Chapter 8 shifts here from ethical commands (vv.16-17) to eschatological promises (vv.19-23) — the ethical demands are grounded in and directed toward the eschatological vision of universal peace that follows.
19: tzom harevi’i vetzom hachamishi vetzom hashvi’i vetzom ha’asiri yihyeh leveit Yehudah lesason ulesimchah ulemo’adim tovim: ‘The fasts of the fourth, fifth, seventh, and tenth months will become occasions of joy and gladness and cheerful festivals.’ The four fasts commemorated specific catastrophes of the Babylonian period (fourth month: Jerusalem breached; fifth: temple burned; seventh: Gedaliah murdered; tenth: siege begun). Their transformation into feasts is the comprehensive eschatological reversal of the exile’s grief: the specific dates of historical disaster become the dates of eschatological celebration. The closing clause — veha-emet veha-shalom ehavu (love truth and peace) — is the ethical foundation for this transformation: the fasts become feasts not automatically but through a community characterized by emet and shalom.
20: od asher yavo’u ammim veyoshevei arim rabbot: ‘Peoples will yet come — inhabitants of many cities.’ The eschatological pilgrimage of the nations to Jerusalem is the OT’s vision of universal worship that runs from Isaiah 2:2-4 (all nations streaming to YHWH’s mountain) through Micah 4:1-3 to this passage. The phrase od asher yavo’u (peoples will yet come) introduces the coming universal movement as still future but certain: the “yet” (od) acknowledges the current partial state of affairs while affirming its ultimate completion.
21: nilchah venelchah el pnei YHWH ulevaqesh et YHWH tzvaot: ‘Let us go immediately to seek the LORD’s favor and to seek the LORD of Hosts.’ The speech among the nations — city inviting city to make pilgrimage — represents the spontaneous spread of the desire to seek YHWH among the Gentiles. The verb chalah pnei (seek the LORD’s favor, literally “soften/entreat the face of”) is a technical term for seeking divine favor, often in crisis (Ps 119:58; Dan 9:13). The urgency of the invitation — nilchah (let us go quickly) — expresses the eschatological eagerness that characterizes the nations’ turning to YHWH.
22: veba’u ammim rabbim vegoyim atzumim livaqesh et YHWH tzvaot biYerushalayim ulechalot et pnei YHWH: ‘Many peoples and powerful nations will come to seek the LORD of Hosts in Jerusalem and to seek the LORD’s favor.’ The escalating scope — many peoples and powerful/numerous nations — makes the pilgrimage universal in reach. The double verb pair (livaqesh to seek + lechalot to seek favor) intensifies the earnestness of the nations’ approach. This verse is the OT background for Isaiah 2:3 (“many peoples shall come and say: Come, let us go up to the mountain of the LORD”) and for the NT’s vision of the church as the gathering-point for all nations (John 12:32: “when I am lifted up, I will draw all people to myself”).
23: koh amar YHWH tzvaot bayamim hahem asher yachaziqu asarah anashim mikol leshonot hagoyim vehecheziku bichnaph ish Yehudi lemor nelchah imachem ki shama’nu Elohim imachem: ‘In those days ten men from every nation and language will take hold of the garment of a Jew, saying, Let us go with you, for we have heard that God is with you.’ The image of ten men from every nation grasping the hem of a Jewish man’s garment is the OT’s most vivid picture of Gentile inclusion through Israel’s witness. Ten is a symbolic number of completeness (a minyan, the quorum for synagogue prayer); “from every nation and language” is universal; “God is with you” (Elohim imachem) is the Immanuel formula (Isa 7:14: God-with-us, Emmanuel) applied to the restored community. The garment-grasping (bichnaph ish, by the hem of a man) is an act of supplication — the nations approaching Israel not with hostility but with petition.
